key_points: Barracks-style living is mandatory for all cadets, no exceptions
Rooms are shared, spartan, and subject to inspection at any time
Living conditions are part of the disciplinary system, not incidental
key_quotes: This entire video takes place during the first 2 days of challenge week... This video does not represent the school well because there is no cadre.
It's been this way for generations. My grandfather went there with this type of welcome, as did my dad 30 years later.
He was a completely different person after knob year.

key_points: Social life is structured around corps activities, not free choice
Brotherhood and unit cohesion replace typical college social scenes
Combat veterans entering as cadets face unique social awkwardness
key_quotes: I can't imagine how this must have felt to the combat veterans going here to go mustang. Imagine being an actual war fighter getting yelled at by these college kids.
LOL, class of '88... we were doing the same dumb shit, but we weren't putting it on video.
everyone here is pretty respectful and I always have fun

key_points: Mess hall attendance is mandatory and highly regimented
Dining is a formal, stressful event — not a casual meal
Alumni note mess was historically 'the most stressful part of the day'
key_quotes: In my day we had a real mess and it was the most stressful part of the day both lunch and evening mess.
And that's what we call Monday.
I'm ready for the challenge of The Citadel!

key_points: Knob year is intentionally brutal — designed to break and rebuild recruits
No mention of accessible mental health resources in any student comment
Transformation is real but at significant psychological cost
key_quotes: He was a completely different person after knob year. Ever since he's been straight laced, buff, deep voiced, no nonsense.
I'm still surprised my brother made it through 4 years of this.
I never even thought of attending. It creates outstanding Cadets.

key_points: Cost cited at ~$30k/year, a serious burden for many students
ROTC scholarships are the primary financial relief strategy mentioned
One student chose a free local school over Citadel due to cost
key_quotes: It is a crazy amount of money to go 30k a year from what I read. I'm applying for a ROTC scholarship but I haven't gotten it yet.
I have also been accepted in a smaller school where I live and can go for free with an amazing ROTC program.
I've been accepted into the Citadel for class of 2022. I wanna ask how did you pay?

key_points: Military college structure leaves limited time for self-directed study
One student reports averaging only ~20 hrs/week study despite 18 credits
Academic rigor exists but competes directly with military obligations
key_quotes: I am in a military college so that eats up my time... My average study time per week has been ~20 hours.
My average credit hours for the last four semesters is 18 credits, meaning 54-72 hours a week [recommended].
I quickly learned how daunting of a task it is to get in that quantity of practice.

key_points: Anti-hazing rules exist on paper but alumni openly doubt enforcement
Confederate flag controversy in chapel acknowledged but contested
Administration presented positively in official videos; reality differs
key_quotes: As an alumnus, I laughed at the part where the Commandant said that hazing would result in expulsion. If that were the case, very few members of the Corps of Cadets would remain.
I also doubt that alumni protested against the Confederate flag being in the chapel.
This video does not represent the school well because there is no cadre.

key_points: Located in Charleston, SC — historic but carrying Confederate legacy
Campus has deep generational traditions tied to Southern military culture
Confederate history is embedded in the institution, not a minor footnote
key_quotes: Ties to the Confederacy? No shit. It's located in Charleston, SC — you know, that place where the secessionist movement began.
The entire South had ties to the Confederacy.
Just got accepted for the Fall 2022 school year. This is the first video that actually gives me some idea of what the Citadel will REALLY be like.

key_points: Citadel grads praised by military officers as more down-to-earth than peers
Strong pipeline into military officer roles, especially Army and Air Force
Graduates noted as exceptionally disciplined and career-ready
key_quotes: Out of all of the mil academy officers I worked with, I enjoyed the Citadel and VMI guys the most. Definitely more down to earth than the others.
My dream is to become an Air Force officer!
Ever since he's been straight laced, buff, deep voiced, no nonsense, hard working model of a soldier and citizen.

key_points: At $30k/year, cost is high for a state military college experience
Free local alternatives with ROTC make Citadel's value hard to justify for some
Transformation and military career pipeline may justify cost for committed students
key_quotes: It is a crazy amount of money to go 30k a year from what I read.
I have also been accepted in a smaller school where I live and can go for free with an amazing ROTC program and good friends.
These kids will be successful, it's good to test yourself and to go against the grain.

key_points: Deeply transformative but only for students who fully commit to the system
Not a college experience — it's a full military lifestyle with a degree attached
Alumni loyalty is intense; prospective students should research before romanticizing
key_quotes: my parents suggested the citadel to me almost as a joke, but I'm looking at this now and lowkey taking it seriously
I'm still not completely clear on what happened toward the end of high school that made him think, 'yeah, this is what I want.'
Best of luck to y'all.

key_points: Hazing: alumni openly admit hazing rules are not enforced in practice
Confederate legacy: Confederate flag in chapel disputed; history not resolved
Cost vs. alternatives: $30k/year with free ROTC options available nearby
key_quotes: As an alumnus, I laughed at the part where the Commandant said that hazing would result in expulsion. If that were the case, very few members of the Corps of Cadets would remain.
I also doubt that alumni protested against the Confederate flag being in the chapel.
I have also been accepted in a smaller school where I live and can go for free with an amazing ROTC program.

key_points: Citadel grads respected in military circles as unusually grounded officers
Generational tradition creates a powerful alumni network and identity
Knob year produces measurable, lasting character change for those who finish
key_quotes: Out of all of the mil academy officers I worked with, I enjoyed the Citadel and VMI guys the most. Definitely more down to earth than the others.
It's been this way for generation. My grandfather went there with this type of welcome, as did my dad 30 years later. It creates outstanding Cadets.
Cadet Tiberghien, if you go on to serve as an officer in the US military, your attitude will cause people to follow you into hell.
